segments.text: Variations from Mendelian Genetics

Variations from Mendelian Genetics
a. Incomplete/ Codominance
b. Multiple alleles
c. Multiple Genes affecting one trait
1)  acting independently
2)  interacting (epistasis)
d. Lethal alleles
e. Pleiotropy
f. Variable Penetrance/Expressivity
g. Linkage
h.  Cytoplasmic inheritance
i. Transposable elements

Codominance-Both
Incomplete dominance-New
Codominance

Codominance
The situation in which the heterozygote exhibits the phenotypes of both homozygotes

Incomplete Dominance
Snapdragons

Incomplete Dominance
When one allele is not completely dominant to another allele so that the heterozygote has a phenotype between that shown in individuals homozygous for either individual allele involved.
Sickle Cell Anemia
Codominant

Incomplete dominanceHbAHbA-Normal. Red blood cells 	never sickleHbSHbS- Severe, often fatal anemia.  	Red blood cells sickle-		shaped.HbAHbS-No anemia. Red blood cells 	look slightly abnormal.  Cells 	sickle only under abnormally low 	oxygen concentrations.

Complimentary gene action
Sweet peas

Epistasis
a form of gene interaction whereby one gene interferes with the phenotypic expression of another non-allelic gene so that the phenotype is governed by the former gene and not by the latter gene.
(Typically fewer than 4 phenotypes in a dihybrid cross).

Lethal genes

General rules in your thinking should be:
If one is dealing with a monohybrid cross, you’ll generally see the ratios as quarters, such as the classic 3:1 ratio. A common modification is  1:2:1, where there is incomplete or co-dominance.  A 2:1 ratio is almost always a lethal where the missing quarter of the progeny die in utero.

General rules in your thinking should be:
-If one is dealing with a dihybrid cross, the ratios will generally be in 16ths.  These would include the classic 9:3:3:1 ratio.  Weird modifications of this that still involve 16ths, such as 9:7, or 13:3, or a 12:3:1, usually involve 2 genes interacting.

Pleiotropy
The production, by one particular mutant gene, of apparently unrelated multiple effects at the phenotypic level.
Pleiotropic Mutation

Genotype		    Phenotype
X
Penetrance The proportion of individuals with a specific genotype who manifest that genotype at the phenotypic level.
Expressivity:The degree to which a particular genotype is expressed in the phenotype.

A human disorder that is recessive produces blindness.  Persons that are homozygous recessive for this disorder however, may lose all of their vision, or only specific segments of their visual field.  What would be the genetic explanation for such a phenomenon?

An autosomal recessive disorder produces extra fingers and toes.  Some victims have only one extra finger, while others have several.  Some people who inherit two recessive alleles have a normal number of fingers and toes. How might this be explained?
